coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots inhibit root growth by disrupting auxin biosynthesis and transport in Arabidopsis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Xiaoyan
Xu, Qiang
Li, Dongxia
Wang, Jianhua
Han, Rong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) possess considerable potentials in fields like biomarker and cell imaging due to its good fluorescence properties. Nevertheless, the molecular mechanism concerning influences of CDs on plant growth still remains unknown. In this study, the subcellular localization of CDs in Arabidopsis and the molecular mechanism of CDs toxicity to plants were investigated. Results demonstrate that CDs tend to accumulate in meristematic nucleus of root tips. CDs can inhibit growth of meristem zone of primary root (PR) of Arabidopsis seedlings significantly. The transcription level of auxin biosynthesis related genes decreases and the abundance of auxin efflux carriers PIN1 and PIN2 declines after 40 mg/L CDs treatment, thus lowering the auxin level in root tips. Moreover, CDs weaken activity of cell division in meristem zone by disturbing expressions of DNA damage repair genes and cell cycle regulation genes, thus enabling to inhibit growth of the meristem zone. To sum up, CDs inhibit growth of Arabidopsis seedlings through above pathways. These results provide useful information to elaborate potential toxicity mechanism of CDs on terrestrial plants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Phytotoxicity
Auxin
Mitosis
Gene expression

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Uptake, transportation and distribution of CDs in Arabidopsis seedlings
Toxic effects of CDs on the growth of Arabidopsis seedlings
CDs disrupt auxin accumulation and transport in root tips
CDs inhibit the cell division of root meristem

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption and distribution of CDs in Arabidopsis. (A) - (C) Fluorescence microscopy images of Arabidopsis seedlings treated with CDs (40 mg/L) for 4 days: (A) Meristematic zone and elongation zone of PR. (B) Root hair zone of PR. (C)Leaf. Scale bar: 200 µm (A,B), 50 µm (C). CDs emissions are shown in blue. Endogenous emission is shown in red (chloroplasts). (D), (E) Confocal images of Arabidopsis seedlings treated with CDs (40 mg/L): (D) vascular bundles in the root hair zone. (E) stomata of a leaf. (F) Confocal images of meristematic cells in Arabidopsis PR exposed to CDs solution (40 mg/L) for 4 h and 14 h. All images were collected under same exposure conditions. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

CDs affect the growth of Arabidopsis seedlings. (A), (B) Five-day-old seedlings were treated with 0, 0.1, 5, 20, and 40 mg/L CDs for 4 days, and PR length was measured. (C), (D) Five-day-old seedlings were treated with 0 (control) and 40 mg/L CDs for 4 days, and The length of apical meristem and elongation region were measured. (C) meristematic zone length, (D) elongation zone length. Bar = 1 cm. Data are shown as mean ±SD. Data presented are average of three replicates. The different letters indicate significantly different values (P < 0.05), and the asterisks indicate significant differences from the control (0 mg/L CDs) (Student’s t-test, P < 0.05).

CDs Disrupt Auxin Accumulation and Transport in Root Tips. (A), (B) The effect of CDs on auxin accumulation in the root tip was monitored using the DR5-GUS lines. GUS activities are shown in the roots of 5-day-old seedlings treated without or with CDs (40 mg/L) for 12 h, 24 h, 48 h. Scale bar: 100 µm. (C) - (F) The effect of CDs treatment for different time points on the abundance of PIN1, PIN2 in root tips. (D), (F) show the quantification of fluorescence intensity in (C), (E) respectively. (G) qRT-PCR analysis of the transcription of auxin biosynthesis-related genes in Arabidopsis thaliana seedling root tips treated with or without CDs (40 mg/L) for 4 days. The expression levels of the indicated genes in untreated seedlings were set to 1. Data are shown as mean ± SD. Data presented are average of three replicates. The different letters indicate significantly different values (P < 0.05), and the asterisks indicate significant differences from the control (0 mg/L CDs) (Student’s t-test, P < 0.05). All images were collected under same exposure conditions.

5 nM IAA can partially restore the inhibition of PR growth induced by CDs. 5-day-old Arabidopsis seedlings were transferred to 1/4 MS media supplemented with 5 nM IAA (3-indoleacetic acid), CDs (40 mg/L) + IAA (5 nM) and CDs (40 mg/L). After 3 days, the PR length was measured. Bar = 1 cm. Data are shown as mean ±SD. Data presented are average of three replicates. The different letters indicate significantly different values (P < 0.05).

CDs inhibited the cell division of root meristem. (A) Effects of CDs on mitotic index of Arabidopsis thaliana root treated without or with CDs (40 mg/L) for 4 days. Image of GUS staining (B) and the GUS activity (C) of 5-day-old CYCLINB1;1-GUS seedlings treated without or with CDs (40 mg/L) for 1 day, 2 days and 3 days. Scale bar: 100 µm. (D) Effects of CDs on Cell cycle related genes expression in root tips of Arabidopsis thaliana treated without or with CDs (40 mg/L) for 4 days. (E) Effects of CDs on DNA damage repair genes expression in root tips of Arabidopsis thaliana treated without or with CDs (40 mg/L) for 4 days. Data are shown as mean ± SD. Data presented are average of three replicates. The different letters indicate significantly different values (P < 0.05), and the asterisks indicate significant differences from the control (0 mg/L CDs) (Student’s t-test, P < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Carbon dots inhibit root growth by disrupting auxin biosynthesis and transport in Arabidopsis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Higher Education Key Laboratory of Plant Molecular and Environmental Stress Response, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen 041004, Shanxi Province, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Higher Education Key Laboratory of Plant Molecular and Environmental Stress Response, Shanxi Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Higher Education Key Laboratory of Plant Molecular and Environmental Stress Response (Shanxi Normal University) in Shanxi Province, Linfen041004, People’s Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Shanxi Normal University, Gongyuan Street No.1, Yaodu, Linfen 041004, Shanxi, People’s Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) possess considerable potentials in fields like biomarker and cell imaging due to its good fluorescence properties. Nevertheless, the molecular mechanism concerning influences of CDs on plant growth still remains unknown. In this study, the subcellular localization of CDs in Arabidopsis and the molecular mechanism of CDs toxicity to plants were investigated. Results demonstrate that CDs tend to accumulate in meristematic nucleus of root tips. CDs can inhibit growth of meristem zone of primary root (PR) of Arabidopsis seedlings significantly. The transcription level of auxin biosynthesis related genes decreases and the abundance of auxin efflux carriers PIN1 and PIN2 declines after 40 mg/L CDs treatment, thus lowering the auxin level in root tips. Moreover, CDs weaken activity of cell division in meristem zone by disturbing expressions of DNA damage repair genes and cell cycle regulation genes, thus enabling to inhibit growth of the meristem zone. To sum up, CDs inhibit growth of Arabidopsis seedlings through above pathways. These results provide useful information to elaborate potential toxicity mechanism of CDs on terrestrial plants.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to small volume and unique physical and chemical properties, nanomaterials have been extensively used in various fields, such as chemistry, medicine, biology, food, textile, agriculture, cosmetics, etc (Nowack and Bucheli, 2007; Yao et al., 2014; Li et al., 2018). Production and marketing of nanomaterials are increased significantly in recent years. People show concerns on the environmental consequences of nanomaterial production and emission levels (Ma et al., 2015). Therefore, it is urgent to understand characteristics of nanomaterials and evaluate their potential risks to biology and environment (Gottschalk et al., 2013; Batley et al., 2013; Chen et al., 2014b).

Among so many nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs), as a new nanomaterial, has the advantages of small particle size, high water solubility, good fluorescence properties, low biotoxicity and good biocompatibility (Yang et al., 2009b; Peng et al., 2012; Mochalin and Gogotsi, 2009). Hence, CDs have been widely applied in photocatalysis, biosensing, drug delivery, especially in biomarker and cell imaging (Tao et al., 2012; Feng et al., 2017; Cheng et al., 2014). With small size and high water solubility, CDs are easier to enter into environment than other nanomaterials. Therefore, evaluating biological toxicity of CDs is of significant importance. Recently, researches on absorption, transportation and toxicity of carbon nanomaterials in vivo mainly focus on animals and microorganisms (Yang et al., 2009b, 2009a; Ray et al., 2009). However, there are very limited studies on absorption, transportation and toxicity of CDs in plants.

Most studies on absorption and transportation of CDs in plants have not involved the subcellular level. Wang et al. reported that CDs can penetrate plant cells of mung beans and then be transported from the root to stems and leaves (Wang et al., 2018). Chen et al. found that CDs were absorbed by root system of Arabidopsis and then transported to leaves through vascular bundles, and mainly distributed in veins (Chen et al., 2018). Therefore, it is necessary to study localization of CDs in cells.

According to previous studies, CDs have both positive and negative effects on plant growth after being absorbed by plants (Verma et al., 2019). For example, water-soluble CDs could promote growths of root system and stems of wheat (Tripathi and Sarkar, 2014). 0.02 mg/mL CDs could accelerate electron transfer rate of photosystem and promote photosynthesis of mung beans, thus increasing carbohydrate content (Wang et al., 2018). The inhibition effect of CDs on photosynthesis and nutrient uptake of scenedesmus obliquus was related with dose and time, and CDs inhibited growth of algae (Yao et al., 2018). The growth of corn seedlings was inhibited significantly after a 4-week treatment of 1000 and 2000 mg/L CDs, resulting in membrane damages, oxidative stress and activation of antioxidase of corn plants (Chen et al., 2016). Qian et al. found that surface modified CDs could be accumulated in root system of pumpkin and transferred to branches, and these CDs can trigger antioxidant defense system of pumpkin seedlings (Qian et al., 2018).

Auxin plays an important role in regulating plant root growth in response to environmental signals. Auxin gradient distribution in root tips is mediated by the biosynthesis and transport of auxin (Yoshihiro, 2012; Grieneisen et al., 2007). The auxin influx carrier and efflux carriers are crucial for maintaining auxin levels (Grieneisen et al., 2007). However, most of previous studies on mechanism of plant biological toxicity of CDs focus on photosynthesis and oxidative stress (Wang et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2018, 2016; Yao et al., 2018; Qian et al., 2018). Whether CDs treatment can disturb synthesis and polar transportation of auxin? Do CDs have influences on cell division and cell cycle of plants? What is the subcellular localization of CDs in plants? There are rare reports on above problems. In this study, these issues were addressed.

The synthesis and dialysis of CDs referred to method of Li et al. (2017). Firstly, eutectic mixture of NaNO3/ KNO3/NaNO2 (mass ratio = 7:53:40 and melting point = 140 ℃) was mixed with citric acid according to the mass ratio of 10:1, and then grinded into fine powder. Next, the fine powder was put into a 220 ℃ heating device and heated for 6 h. The mixture was then cooled to room temperature and then dissolved with water. CDs solution was filtered by a piece of 0.1 mm filter paper, followed by 3-day dialysis (MW = 1000) to eliminate salts and low-molecular-weight products. Finally, CDs were treated by freeze drying and powder was gained.

The morphology of CDs was characterized via transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100F TEM, Japan). The ultraviolet / visible spectra were detected by UV-29100 UV–vis spectrometer (Varian Inc., CA, USA). The fluorescence spectra were collected by Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian American Pty Ltd., USA). Characterization of CDs was shown in the Supporting information Fig. S1.

This study applied wild-type Col-0, CYCB1;1:CYCB1;1-GUS, DR5-GUS, PIN1:PIN1-GFP and PIN2:PIN2-GFP plants of Arabidopsis (Colon-Carmona et al., 1999; Ulmasov, 1997; Santelia et al., 2008). Firstly, seeds were disinfected by 1.5% sodium hypochlorite and then sowed onto 1/4 MS culture media for vertical culture (16 h under light and 8 h in dark, 22 °C). Later, the 5-day-old seedlings were transferred to 1/4 MS medium with different concentrations of CDs and cultured for another 1–4 days. Root length, meristematic zone length and elongation zone length were measured by Image J software. Mitotic Index = Number of mitotic cells/number of observed cells × 100%.

GUS staining referred to previous methods (Stangeland and Salehian, 2002), and then observed and photographed with a microscope (OLYMPUS, BX53). Pictures of localization of CDs in plant tissues were taken and observed under a fluorescence microscope (OLYMPUS, BX53). Localization of CDs in vascular bundles, stoma and cells were observed under a laser scanning confocal microscope (OLYMPUS, FV1000) (excitation wavelength = 405 nm). A laser scanning confocal microscope was applied for observation of GFP and the excitation wavelength chose 488 nm. Activity of GUS was quantified with references to the method of Beziat et al. (2017). GFP fluorescence intensity of PR was tested by Image J software.

5-day-old Arabidopsis seedlings grown on 1/4 MS medium were transferred to 1/4 MS medium supplemented with 40 mg/L CDs for 4 days. Roots (0.1 g) was collected and total RNA was extracted by the plant RNA extraction kit TransZol Up Plus RNA Kit (Transgen), and then synthesized into cDNA by the inverse transcription kit TransScript One-step gDNA Removal and cDNA Synthesis SuperMix (Transgen). Quantitative real-time-PCR (qRT-PCR) reaction was performed by PerfectStart Green qPCR SuperMix (Transgen) kit and Applied Biosystems QuantStudio 3. ACTIN7 was applied as the internal controls of qRT-PCR. The relative expressions of genes were calculated by the formula 2 –ΔΔCT (Livak and Schmittgen, 2002). Primer sequences are listed in Supporting information Table S1.

The experiment was repeated three times under the same conditions. At least 30 seedlings were analyzed in each repeat. Values are presented as the means ± SD. SPSS18.0 software was used for variance analysis to detect the significance of the difference (P < 0.05). The column chart was made by Graphpad Prism5.0.

It can be seen from transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CDs (Fig. S1A) that CDs are spheres in uniform size and particle diameter is smaller than 4 nm, showing good dispersion. Fig. S1B displays that the particle size distribution is about 1.2–3.6 nm. Fig. S1C shows the PL and UV-Vis absorption spectra. The purple curves are PL (Photoluminescence) spectra of CDs at excitation of 360 nm, indicating the good fluorescence performance of CDs. Peaks of UV-Vis absorption spectra of CDs solution (red curves) concentrate at 280 nm. The water solution of CDs (right) presents bright light fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm UV-lights (inset in Fig. S1C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake, transportation and distribution of CDs in Arabidopsis seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: The 5-day-old Arabidopsis seedlings were transplanted onto 1/4 MS fresh media with 40 mg/L CDs and cultured for another four days. CDs present blue florescence under UV light. Hence, this study observed fluorescence of roots and leaves of Arabidopsis treated with CDs to determine absorption, transportation and distribution of CDs. It can be seen clearly from Fig. 1A, B that CDs have entered into the meristem zone, elongation zone and root-hair zone of PR, accompanied with a few distributions in veins and mesophyll cells (Fig. 1C). Fluorescent signals cannot be seen in control group. According to observations of laser scanning confocal microscope, clear blue fluorescence in the vascular area of root-hair zone proves that CDs have entered into the vascular bundles (Fig. 1D), indicating that CDs can transport upward through vascular bundles. In addition, there are evident CDs distributions at leave stoma (Fig. 1E). Based on above results, CDs can enter into roots and transported to different parts of Arabidopsis seedlings.

To determine the subcellular distribution of CDs, the roots of 7-day-old Arabidopsis seedlings were immersed in 40 mg/L CDs solution and the subcellular distribution of CDs in meristem zone was observed by a laser scanning confocal microscope every hour. It can be seen from Fig. 1F that CDs which emit blue fluorescence gathered in cell nucleus after 4 h of treatment. As the treatment continues, distribution range of CDs was expanded and CDs accumulation in cytoplasm and cell walls was observed. Cell nucleus in meristem zone is still the main distribution zone of CDs (Fig. 1F).

Root is the first organ that contacts with CDs under the CDs stress. Therefore, whether CDs can be absorbed by roots and transported to other positions is the premise of toxicity effects of CDs on plants. This study proved that CDs can be absorbed by roots of Arabidopsis and transported to leaves through vascular bundles. This is similar with previous research results (Wang et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2018; Li et al., 2016).

Plants transport water and nutrient ions through the symplastic pathway and apoplastic pathway (Steudle and Peterson, 1998). We need to know by what pathways CDs transport in plants. Li et al. believed that CDs were transported to stems and leaves through the apoplastic pathway after being absorbed by mung beans, finally entering into veins rather than mesophyll system (Li et al., 2016). Recently, Chen et al. proposed similar opinions: CDs were absorbed by roots of Arabidopsis and then transported to leaves through vascular bundles, but CDs mainly distributed in veins (Chen et al., 2018). These two articles both believed that transportation of CDs depended on the apoplastic pathway. Nevertheless, this study found that CDs can accumulate in nucleus of meristem zone in root tips, indicating that transportation of CDs in Arabidopsis can be realized through symplastic pathway in addition to the apoplastic pathway. Our results further demonstrate the application value of CDs in cellular imaging and even subcellular imaging of plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxic effects of CDs on the growth of Arabidopsis seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: The 5-day-old Arabidopsis wild-type Col-0 seedlings were transplanted to fresh culture media with different concentrations of CDs and then cultured for another 4d. Compared to the control group, PR growth is inhibited significantly with the increase of CDs concentration ( Fig. 2A, B). 40 mg/L CDs significantly inhibit the growth of PR by 55%. Hence, we chose this concentration in the follow-up experiment.

To further elaborate inhibition effect of CDs on PR growth, meristematic zone length and elongation zone length were measured. The meristematic zone length and elongation zone length of the 40 mg/L CDs treatment group are 28% and 49% lower than those of the control group (Fig. 2C and D). These results suggest that CDS can inhibit cell division and cell elongation in root tips.

Moreover, Fig. S2A and B shows that 40 mg/L CDs inhibit root fresh weight and shoot fresh weight of Arabidopsis seedlings by 55% and 24%, respectively. This reflects that CDs inhibit the growth of underground part more compared to overground part.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs disrupt auxin accumulation and transport in root tips
ce:section.ce:para: Auxin is a key hormone in plant roots growth and plays an important role in the maintenance of root meristem. DR5-GUS transgenic line is a good tool to study the distribution and function of auxin in plants (Ulmasov, 1997). Auxin accumulation in root tips was studied using DR5-GUS transgenic line in order to elaborate whether CDs inhibit PR growth by disturbing accumulations of axuin in roots. The DR5-GUS activity of roots at 12 h of 40 mg/L CDs treatment had no significant changes compared to that of the control group. However, expressions of DR5-GUS of roots decreased significantly at 24 h and 48 h of 40 mg/L CDs treatment ( Fig. 3A and B). The results indicate that the inhibition of root meristem development mediated by CDs may be due to the decrease of auxin concentration in root tips.

Local auxin biosynthesis in roots is essential to normal development of the root system. YUCCA genes (YUC3, YUC5, YUC7, YUC8 and YUC9) are responsible for synthesis of auxin in root systems (Chen et al., 2014a). In this study, we concern whether CDs treatment can influence expression of auxin biosynthesis related genes. Transcript level of auxin biosynthesis related genes of Arabidopsis was tested by qRT-PCR (Fig. 3G). CDs inhibit expressions of 7 auxin biosynthesis related genes (YUC3, YUC5, YUC9, CYP79B2, ASA1, SUR1 and AAO3) significantly, while the expression of AMI1 present no great changes. Transcript levels of most auxin biosynthesis related genes decrease, which reduce accumulation of auxin in root tips. This might be one of reasons that CDs inhibit growth of PR.

Transport carriers of auxin can adjust auxin distribution in root systems (Laskowski et al., 2008; Li et al., 2015; Yuan et al., 2013). Among these transport carriers, the auxin efflux carriers PIN are essential for polar transportation of auxin (Wisniewska et al., 2006). PIN1 and PIN2 are crucial to control cell division, root length and size of meristem (Blilou et al., 2005). To verify whether PIN1 and PIN2 have participated in CDs-mediated inhibition of auxin accumulation, expressions of auxin carriers were detected using reporter lines PIN1:PIN1-GFP and PIN2:PIN2-GFP. Fluorescence intensity of PIN1-GFP declines significantly at 3d of 40 mg/L CDs treatment and it recovered to normal level at 4 d (Fig. 3C and D). The abundance of PIN2-GFP began to decrease at 2 d and it remained significantly lower compared to that of the control group after 4d of CDs treatment (Fig. 3E and F). These results indicated that CDs inhibit protein abundances of PIN1 and PIN2, thus decreasing auxin levels, leading to inhibition of PR growth.

The above results indicated that the change of auxin accumulation level caused by CDs treatment was probably one of the reasons for the inhibition of PR growth. To verify this inference, 5-day-old Arabidopsis seedlings were transferred to 1/4 MS media supplemented with 5 nM IAA (3-indoleacetic acid) (Chen et al., 2014a), CDs + IAA and CDs respectively. After 3 days, the PR length was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4, exogenous application of 5 nM IAA can partially restore the inhibition of PR growth induced by CDs. This suggests that the decrease of auxin in PR is one of the reasons that CDs inhibits the growth of PR.

It is reported that Manganese toxicity disturbs biosynthesis of auxin of Arabidopsis and downregulates expressions of PIN4 and PIN7, thus inhibiting growth of PR (Zhao et al., 2017). Wan et al. found that auxin level in root tips of Arabidopsis seedlings dropped significantly after 3-day treatment by ZnO Nanoparticles (Wan et al., 2019). Our results show that, toxicity of CDs leads to reduction of auxin biosynthesis and abundances of PIN1 and PIN2 at root tips, which further decreases accumulations of auxin in roots and finally inhibit growth of PR. However, how CDs cause reductions of abundances of PIN1 and PIN2 has to be further studied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs inhibit the cell division of root meristem
ce:section.ce:para: Since CDs can inhibit meristem growth in root tips (Fig. 2C), it is speculated that CDs inhibit mitosis of cells in the meristem zone of root tips. Therefore, mitotic index in root tips of Arabidopsis after 4 d of 40 mg/L CDs treatment was tested. It can be seen from Fig. 5A that mitotic index in root tips after CDs treatment is 28.8% lower compared to that of the control group, indicating that toxicity of CDs inhibits mitosis of cells in root tips. Activity of mitosis in meristem zone of root tips determines growth of PR. This study carried out a histochemical staining analysis on expressions of CYCB1;1:CYCB1;1-GUS to observe cell division activity in meristem zone of root tips intuitively. CycB1;1 is a cell cycle protein and expressions of CycB1;1 is used to indicate cell division activity and G2/M phase transition in root tips (Colon-Carmona et al., 1999). The activity of CYCB1;1-GUS begins to decrease after 1 d of CDs treatment and it remains significantly lower compared to that of the control group after 3 d of treatment (Fig. 5B and C). Therefore, CDs inhibit cell division activity in the meristem zone.

In this study, expressions of several cell cycle regulation genes and DNA damage response genes were tested to further determine influences of CDs on cell cycle and DNA damage repair. According to Fig. 5D, expressions of E2Fa (G1/S marker gene) and CYCB1;1 (G2/M marker gene) in 40 mg/L CDs treatment of Arabidopsis decrease by 51.0% and 41.8%, respectively. CDs also inhibit expressions of DNA damage response genes RAD51, MSH2 and MSH6 (Fig. 5E). Our results demonstrate that CDs are easier to be accumulated in cell nucleus in the meristem zone in root tips (Fig. 1F). Therefore, it is speculated that CDs which entered into cell nucleus cause DNA damages and thereby induce cell cycle retardation at G1/S phase or / and G2/M phase. This might be a protection mechanism that prevents cells with damaged DNA from dividing under CDs stress.

There are a lot of researches on cell cycle retardation caused by DNA damage in mammals, but only few knowledge on plant cells is available (Hu et al., 2016; Adachi et al., 2011). Cui et al. found that 2.5 mg/L cadmium stress can decrease expressions of DNA damage response genes and cell cycle related genes in root tips of Arabidopsis significantly, thus inhibiting growth of roots (Cui et al., 2017). This is similar with our results. Therefore, we suggest that CDs weaken cell division activity in the meristem zone by disturbing expressions of DNA damage repair genes and inhibiting expressions of cell cycle regulation genes, finally inhibiting growth of roots.

To sum up, CDs can be absorbed by roots of Arabidopsis and then transported to leaves through vascular bundles. It finds through subcellular localization that CDs can accumulate in meristematic nucleus of root tips. 40 mg/L CDs treatment inhibits growth of PR greatly. The toxicity mechanism of CDs to plants is investigated at the molecular level. Due to toxicity of CDs, transcript levels of most auxin biosynthesis related genes decrease and the abundances of PIN1 and PIN2 are decreased, thus reducing accumulation of auxin in roots. Additionally, CDs can reduce cell division activity of roots by interfering with expressions of DNA damage repair genes and inhibiting expressions of cell cycle regulation genes. CDs inhibit growth of Arabidopsis seedlings through above pathways. This study preliminarily explores signal pathways and molecular mechanisms of CDs regulating PR growth in Arabidopsis, and lays foundations for further exploring the molecular mechanism of CDs phytotoxicity.

Xiaoyan Yan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Qiang Xu: Resources, Data curation, Visualization. Dongxia Li: Formal analysis, Visualization, Investigation. Jianhua Wang : Data curation, Validation. Rong Han: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, 30671061). Thanks to Miao Yanming, Dong Jiayi, Yin Xiao and Liu Shuai for their help in CDs synthesis and characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.ecoenv.2021.112168.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
